Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Hello, I'm Julia. Welcome to the Mind Matters panel.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Pam Leo once said,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: let's raise children who won't have to recover from their childhood.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Hmm, good advice.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But if we're going to do the same thing with teenagers,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: we're going to need a resilient strategy.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: To help us figure out which one to choose, I'm joined by

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Clinical Psychologist and Family Therapist Andrew Fuller,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Teacher Nairisa Rodriguez,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Assistant Principal George Mazaris,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and Teacher Martin DeClurek.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But of course, before we get started,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: let's see what the staff at Eagleton have come up with.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: People say a resilience program is an magic bullet.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Well clearly, those people haven't tried Belgian fire walking.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I first saw it in a TED talk and I immediately ordered the whole kit.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: You should have seen people's faces.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I mean, you use the term fire pit and people immediately think of fire license,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: first-aid training, but in comparison to some other resilience programs,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: this one is absolutely easy.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: All you need is a three by five space lighter fluid and a rake.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: They literally go from caring in fear to racing across these flesh-eating coals

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: like little terminators.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: It's inspiring.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Cameron had a little mishap in the fire pit.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: That hill bounce right back, won't you, Cameron?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: It's all good.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: What a great program.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Well, it still hurts.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Did those students have their feed-in ice buckets?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Phil's thought of everything.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Where can I find his TED talk?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I hope you enjoy my pineapple earrings.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: We're going to talk seriously now for a little while.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Now, Belgian fire walking isn't for every school,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: so what programs are out there and how do you choose the right one?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Love the outfit, by the way. Thank you.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: It's good suiting you.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Well, the first thing we know is that there are 40 key predictors

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: that predict residents and good outcomes for kids.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And they can be loosely grouped into eight major areas.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And so the first thing we really need to do

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: is to assess where the kids stand

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: in terms of their strengths in those areas.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: The first one is connected to adults,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the strength of relationships in the school,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: or the community.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: The second one is empowerment.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: The third one really is boundaries and expectations.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Do we expect much for you?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Have they got clear boundaries in the family?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Then, of course, there's school engagement.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: There's values, there's social skills,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and there's personal identity.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And having obviously each school each year level

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: will vary in terms of which ones are strong

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and which ones are not quite so strong.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And then it's a task, really,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: of thinking about how do we best capitalize

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: on the strengths of our students to increase those,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: to remedy some of the areas that are not so strong?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So, in answer to your question,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: it's not so much any specific program,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: it's more really identifying in that group of kids,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: what are those strengths that I can call upon,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: what are those vulnerabilities I need to address

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and planning it from there?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And often, from that, basically, you start to get the answer.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: What are the challenges of using a bolt-on resilience program

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: in isolation?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: If you go searching for a program and get one,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: it may not be catered directly to the school,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and what you're targeting in resilience might be different.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So, if you get the one program sort of a one-stop shop

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: or one fixes all, it may not be targeting what you need.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But it's been working for you at your school.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: It has, yeah, the challenge we had is it can't look like tokenism,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: so you can't just fix something by implementing a program.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: It has to be part of the culture of the school team.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So, we do a lot of work, not just the program,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: but also in terms of changing the language staff use

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and inside the classroom.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So, we have set times for them doing sort of wellness stuff

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and resiliency stuff,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: but I think it's the more important time

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and the more effective stuff is done outside

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: when they've taken that and implemented it in other situations,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: whether it's in other classes or a recess or a lunchtime one.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I think getting everyone on board too,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: getting all your staff to go with you,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: because if you have sections of your school,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: not having to deal with it on a day-to-day basis,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: it doesn't fly through, right, through your school.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: If you want your staff to be all on board

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and all sort of practicing it in some way

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and building those relationships with the kids.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: What are the benefits of integrating a resilience program

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: into the curriculum?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I mean, the benefits are that you get benefits

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: in the behavior, cooperation, the atmosphere in the class,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: the work ethic of the students and the students themselves

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: that are well-being, which is the most important thing

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: that you're after.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I think what's valued in schools is what's measured.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And so, unfortunately, we live in a nation

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: that measures literacy and numeracy

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and not terribly much else.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I think what we really do need to do is to think about

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: how do we measure students' resilience and well-being

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and have that as one of the clear outcomes for all schools.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And the way to achieve that can be a kind of continuous program,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: as Martin said, or other schools sort of will do it

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: in very different ways.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And I think that's fine.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: There's many, many ways to achieve the same outcomes.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Some schools will decide we're busy as bumblebees

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: in terms one and four.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So we're not going to try and add anything more

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: because we're exhausted at that stage.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: But in terms two and three, we're on a program then.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Or other schools say, well, we're not going to do a program

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: as a continuous kind of stream of sort of sessions.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: We all basically run festivals.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: We'll have a festival of friendship,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: we'll have a festival of ideas,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and we'll have a festival of not knowing.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And it's really, it's horses for courses here.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: It's really thinking about how do you achieve that outcome.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: But the best, the best resilience initiatives,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: it's a bit like creating a tapestry.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And so it's almost like it's woven in

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: to the very fabric of the school culture.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So it affects everybody.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And everyone sees that it benefits their life

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: as well as their life of people around them.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And it changes the relationships between people in that school.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: The festival's idea sounds great fun.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I love that. But just because it's fun,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: does that mean it's actually doing anything?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Are they, is it working?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Or is it just, oh, we get this day where it's fun?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But I think it does.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And our program, well, I found it very difficult

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: to bring a program into our busy curriculum.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So with the student programs that I've sort of led,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I've done it in their time lunchtime.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And we get together, we meet then,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and then we build these ideas that we want to sort of

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: promote throughout the school in the times

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: when kids are out in the yard.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Anyone can. So I avoided the constraints of a time table

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and used the off time.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And it's surprising how many kids don't want to go

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and have their lunchtime.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: They'll come along and have a chat with you

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and want to organise things.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And then you can build it into free dress days, festivals,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: after school events, camps and all the other things

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: that you can do outside of your curriculum.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: What does the resilience program look like at your school, Martin?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: At the moment, we've got sort of two levels.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: We've got a life skills subject.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And so we do the education about stress and well-being

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and things like meditation and also explore

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: stress relief activities for kids.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So they can try different things from circus skills

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: to zumba and that's the official stuff

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: where they kind of do some surveys and stuff

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and work out where they're resilient to years.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And then we also have a senior programme

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: that we've just started this year to target it officially

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: because they don't do the life skills subject.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So we've got that curriculum base.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But then we've got the unofficial witches

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: where it's become part of the culture of the school.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Well, we know here 11 and 12 are incredibly vulnerable years.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: We know that about 20% of students in year 12

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: have some suicidal thoughts during that year.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So it's a very, very...

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Is that the pressure just of year 12 that brings that up?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Am I going to get the marks that I want?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Am I going to get into the course that I want or the job that I want?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So it's an incredibly vulnerable time.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So much so that we really have to rethink our model

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: around well-being, particularly at year 11 and year 12.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Obviously, critically is helping parents as well as students

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: understand how to succeed at school is important.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: That journey often begins around the middle of year 10

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: is often the time when schools really need to reconnect

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: with the parents that they've lost along the way.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: But the other thing is to really think about how you utilize

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: your past students as well.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And so in a number of areas of Australia

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: who have had past students who've mentored current students

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: but just seeing a future pathway beyond these marks

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: that kids get so focused on is really helpful, I think.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: People want to see results, don't they?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: How do you know that these programs are working?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: What results would you expect to see, Andrew?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Oh, when we've surveyed over 22,000 young Australians

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: in terms of this, and you do see incremental shifts

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: in terms of those eight key areas that I mentioned earlier

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: as schools embark upon initiatives.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: But beyond the stats, the thing that really shows

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: me that it's working in a school,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: which might sometimes surprise you,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: firstly, the level of energy in the schools lifts.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And so people kind of look brighter eyed,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: but the other real key indicator is that forgiveness creeps

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: into a school.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Forgiveness is a critical key in all of this

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: because I suppose the results of those 22,000 surveys

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: that we've done tell us that young Australians

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: are quite good at forming relationships.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: What they're not very good at is when a relationship

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: falls upon tough times, how do you patch it up?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And so it's far easier to do a geographical,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I'm not talking to you again, if I had a falling out and move on,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: rather than go, hang on, well, maybe, you know,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Martin didn't really mean that,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: maybe I need to kind of have a chat and sort of out.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Is that cultural? Do you think that's something

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: particularly that it happens in Australia

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: or would it happen in other countries, do you think?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I think it certainly happens around the world,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: but I think Australians do have a bit of a cut and run

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: kind of mentality.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Yeah, I don't know, I'm genuinely, yeah, it's very interesting.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I think maybe our convict origins are part of our family.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Well, we often split off from our families,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: many of us not all of us and have come out here

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: either as refugees or as convicts or as new settlers.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And so it has been a kind of pioneering

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: individualistic kind of model.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Another one is attendance,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: attendance from staff and students.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: From staff?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: There's an upside-down look.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: All right, I'll go in today.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But you can probably vouch for this,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: the amount of sort of mental health days or the staff

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: to reduce with that.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I think teachers should just have to do four days a week.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And then they have always had three days off

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and then they'd probably be a little bit more have the energy

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: because it's a big job.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: We've got our data that we get every year from our students.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So we go and survey them

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and they come back to us with a range of responses.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But one of the things that we've known

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: since we've started to put a bit of time

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: into some of our programs is that kids feel safe

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: at our school and connected to their school.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: We've worked on the attendance issues that you talked about.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But they also have improved and say in the last three years.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So yeah, the safety for us has really improved

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: in a space of three years

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and the kids are telling us that.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: We've had significant change.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: From 2008, we had 1,950 suspension days,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: which is equivalent to 10 years.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And in 2011, when the cafe started,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: we were down to 350 at the end of 2011.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: That's fantastic. That's phenomenal.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But you also do PBIS.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: How does PBIS work?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So it's recognising positive behaviour

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and for each time you are recognised

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: for positive behaviour, you get a merit card

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: or we actually use online modelling system

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: where we can just tick and flick box

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: to say, Mohamed's done a great job today

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and he's been a safe, respectful learner

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and that goes on his record.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Once he gets to 10, he gets a year-advisor award.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Once he gets to three lots of year-advisor awards,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: he goes on excursion.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So where do we find these resilience programmes

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and where can we find the good ones, the evidence-based ones?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Well, for the information for our life skills subjects,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: the resiliency stuff,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: actually swapped what we're doing with our school

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: with someone that I met at a PD from another school

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and we just exchanged our programmes

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and talked about what worked and what didn't work.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So the best ideas you steal from other people?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I'm a big fan of stealing.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: You should always steal.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Yeah, because it's dull and all that.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Nothing's original. You steal all the time.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Yeah, youth workers in our community have often brought ideas along

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and then we've gone and asked other schools

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: how they work with them.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So comparing notes with local schools.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Our multi-pride programme is in maybe five or six schools

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: started off in one school, adopted by others

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and each school is probably ran with it slightly differently.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: They're all student-led,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: but the kids pick the types of issues

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: that are important to their school.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So multi-pride in one school

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: might be slightly different to the other,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: except that they do meet

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and they talk about student issues.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And you can you find these things online?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Yes, I mean, alongside the Beyond Blue website, obviously.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And the Mind Matters website?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And the Mind Matters website.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: There's also, I manage a couple of Facebook pages

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: where I collect ideas.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: One's called Resident Youth Australia,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: where I put up ideas that are abused by schools all around

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the country and overseas in terms of resilience initiatives

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: which have basis in research.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And another one which is for teachers,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: which is all of the stuff on neuroscience

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and learning how brains work

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and activities for classrooms

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: called the Learning Brain.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I've actually downloaded information

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: about, to educate students about sleep hygiene

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and stuff from Andrew's website too.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So there's a lot of, yeah.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: You talked a lot about sleep yesterday.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: We loved it.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I got given a sleeping bangle.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Good.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But I don't need this quietly.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: There are many, many residents sort of programs

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and initiatives and strategies

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and the fit between the program and your school

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: has to be pretty tight.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So you've really got to think about,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: as George said,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: what's going to bring most of the people on side?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: You need to think about things that are going to rev up

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and kind of have a sense of meaning

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and compassion as Martin said.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: It's taking something that has clearly

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the capacity to show you a pre-measure,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: not just of the problems,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: but also the strengths of students

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and then give you the capacity to re-measure

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: maybe 12 months down the track.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So you can kind of see.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: We know that we're not going to tackle

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: all of the resilience issues in one 12 month period

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: but we want to have a different profile

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: in a year's time than we have now.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Some areas will have increased.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Hopefully not too many have decreased

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: but at the same time,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: what will be tackling probably a year from today

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: should be different than what we're tackling this year

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: because there will be different priorities

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and that also applies to different age groups as well.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Andrew brought up just reminding me

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: that it's really important to review too

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and to keep reviewing

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and you can't just implement a program and go problem solved.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: We're done now.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: You need to keep checking back and reviewing

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: how it's going and tweaking it.

Text: And the Mind Matter Survey can help with that.
